- An out-of-benchmark stake in U.S. Treasurys - which the fund held, along with cash, for liquidity and hedging purposes - hampered the fund's relative result in Q2. (Negative)
- Underweighting other governmentrelated debt, especially from Panama and Mexico, also hurt. (Negative)
- In contrast, non-benchmark exposure to high-yield corporate bonds contributed to relative performance. (PoSitive)
Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve
Market Environment The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened during the quarter, with rates falling at the front end and rising at the long end.
Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark) Yield-curve positioning - particularly in the intermediate-maturity portion of the curve - aided relative performance this quarter. (PoSitive)
Strategy: Security Selection
Market Environment Among maturities, intermediate bonds fared best while long-term issues declined. By credit quality, securities rated BBB outperformed higher-quality investment-grade bonds.
Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)
Security selection contributed to the fund's relative performance.
- Within industrials, bond selection among communications, pharmaceutical and automotive firms helped, led by Charter Communications, Bayer and General Motors. (PoSitive)
- In financials, picks among REITs added the most value, specifically, Hudson Pacific Properties and Sun Communities. (PoSitive)

Outlook and Positioning

As of June 30, the risks of U.S. tariff policy hampering economic growth, and of fiscal policy driving U.S. Treasury yields higher, remain. However, market participants seem content to look past these risks for now, owing to favorable economic data, less-strident recent tariff rhetoric and a shift in the administration's focus to the more stimulative elements of fiscal policy.

Inflation remains benign, with the core consumer price index holding steady at 2.7% year-over-year. Meanwhile, the labor market is resilient, with June payrolls and the unemployment rate coming in better than expected. That said, due to lingering tariff uncertainty, companies remain in a wait-and-see mode with respect to potentially raising prices or cutting headcount. The Federal Reserve acknowledges the upside risk to inflation and the potential for employment to weaken. However, it is waiting in an effort to get a clearer sense of the impact of tariffs on the broader economy.

With respect to Fed rate cuts, market participants are pricing in two reductions by the end of 2025, partly driven by speculation that President Trump may soon announce a more-dovish successor to Chairman Powell.

The August 1 deadline for the administration's proposed "reciprocal" tariffs may provide some clarity, but with further extensions possible, the full impact on corporate earnings and the economic growth outlook remains to be seen.

Corporate issuance totaled $104 billion in June, down roughly $50 billion from May but in line with estimates. Gross supply is running ahead of 2024's pace but continues to be well-digested by the market. Net flows into investment-grade bond funds were positive for the quarter, but slowed in June, partly due to falling Treasury yields, along with strong performance in broader risk markets. All told, supply/demand dynamics remain a tailwind for the market, in our view, with manageable gross issuance, reasonably attractive yields and net inflows to retail funds.

Within this context, we plan to maintain a moderate overweight to credit risk, with slightly less exposure to shorter-maturity securities versus the benchmark. We continue to favor select issuers rated BBB where we see the opportunity for alpha potential over time resulting from improving credit quality. We plan to maintain an overweight allocation in the financials sector, emphasizing banks, insurance companies and REITs.

We also expect to keep the portfolio underweighted in governmentrelated debt and in the industrials sector, where we are focused on careful security selection. We plan to retain a meaningful liquidity cushion of U.S. Treasurys and cash in the portfolio, as we believe this "dry powder" will enable us to capitalize on compelling credit opportunities as they arise. As of June 30, roughly 7% of the fund's assets were allocated to Treasurys and about 1% were in cash. ■

Fidelity Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:FCOR) Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Corporate Bond ETF is a credit-focused bond strategy that seeks a high level of current income.
  • Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance commensurate with investor expectations of a primarily investment-grade corporate bond fund.
  • Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including security selection, industry allocation and opportunistic trading.
  • Robust governance and risk management - consisting of extensive quantitative modeling, formal and informal portfolio reviews, and proprietary tools - support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC restated yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

30-day SEC yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30 -day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund; determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

FUND RISKS

In general the bond market, especially foreign markets, are volatile, and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Fixed income securities carry interest rate risk. (As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer-term securities.) Fixed income securities also carry inflation risk and credit and default risks for both issuers and counterparties. Unlike individual bonds, most bond funds do not have a maturity date, so avoiding losses caused by price volatility by holding them until maturity is not possible Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency-exchange-rate, economic, and political risks. Investments in mortgage securities are subject to the risk that principal will be repaid prior to maturity. As a result, when interest rates decline, gains may be reduced, and when interest rates rise, losses may be greater. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk. The fund generally expects to effect its creations and redemptions for cash rather than in-kind securities, and may recognize more capital gains and be less tax-efficient than if it were to redeem in-kind. An ETF may trade at a premium or discount to its Net Asset Value (NAV). There can be no assurance that an active trade market will be maintained and trading may be halted due to market conditions.

INDICES

Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index is a market-value-weighted index of investment-grade corporate fixed-rate debt issues with maturities of one year or more.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market-value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. PERFORMANCE SUMMARY

Net Asset Value (NAV) Return represents the closing price of underlying securities at which the fund can create and redeem shares directly with the security. Market Return represents the price at which an investor can buy and sell a security in the secondary market. Since ETFs are bought and sold at prices set by the market - which can result in a premium or discount to NAV - the returns calculated using market price (market return) can differ from those calculated using NAV (NAV return).

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. RSquared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3 -month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returnsover time.Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

TRADING INFORMATION

Bid/Ask Spread

In the case of exchange-traded securities, market liquidity is managed by the market maker community. Market makers provide prices at which they will buy (the bid) and sell (the ask) securities. The difference between the bid and ask prices is the bid/ask spread. The bid/ask spread gives an investor a sense of the liquidity and the transaction costs associated with a security. Securities with wide bid/ask spreads tend to be less liquid and have higher cost of ownership. Those with tight bid/ask spreads tend to be more liquid and have a lower cost of ownership.

Premium/Discount

The exchange-quoted price of an ETF frequently differs from the ETF's underlying value. The difference in these values represents an ETF's discount or premium. In the case of a discount, the exchange-quoted share price is lower than the ETF's underlying value. An ETF is at a premium when the exchange-quoted price is greater than its underlying value. An ETF's discount or premium fluctuates over time and is influenced by such factors as the transaction costs of the underlying holdings, the cost of creation and redemption of shares, and the average daily volume. The magnitude and volatility of an ETF's premium/discount will affect an investor's holding period return on an ETF. If an investor buys shares at a discount and sells at a premium, they should outperform the NAV return of the ETF. On the other hand, if an investor buys at a premium and sells at a discount, they should underperform the NAV return of the ETF.

YIELD CURVE

The relationship at a given point in time between yields on a group of fixed-income securities with varying maturities commonly, Treasury bills, notes, and bonds. The curve typically slopes upward since longer maturities normally have higher yields, although it can be flat or even inverted.

data.title: Community Bank Earnings Stand Up To Tariffs For Now
data.description:
US community banks' strong expansion in net interest margins has bolstered earnings in the first half of 2025, but the delayed impact of tariffs will surface in the coming quarters and slow loan growth and push credit costs higher.

The take

Modest declines in funding costs and the repricing of fixed rate assets from the pandemic era has fueled margin expansion at community banks and double-digit earnings growth through the first six months of the year. Margins are still expected to expand further, but earnings growth should not be as strong in the second half of 2025 as the lagging impact of tariffs slow growth, weigh on the consumer and push delinquencies higher and require banks to build reserves. Those modest earnings headwinds should provide further support for stronger bank M&A activity as institutions seek ways to grow.

Fixed asset repricing bolsters community bank margins

Community banks will continue to benefit from remixing their balance sheets as lower-yielding securities mature and are reinvested at higher yields, while fixed rate loan yields originated during the pandemic roll off and are replaced with new credits carrying more attractive yields.

Declines in deposit costs have also supported margin expansion in recent quarters, but the pace of decline has slowed. Community banks' cost of deposits dipped 1 basis point quarter-over-quarter in the second quarter. That decrease pales in comparison to the 17 basis points linked quarter decline in the first quarter and 7 basis points quarter-over-quarter decrease in the fourth quarter. Even while holding the line on the deposit costs, community banks failed to attract new funds in the second quarter as deposit balances actually declined in the period.

Community banks recorded significant pricing relief in prior quarters because they shed higher cost CDs originated when deposit competition was far fiercer. Pricing on CDs has significantly influenced deposit costs since community banks relied heavily on CDs to bolster their funding during the recent tightening cycle.

Banks grew CDs as deposit pricing competition reached a fever pitch in the last six months of 2023 and the early part of 2024. That period followed the liquidity crunch in the spring of 2023 that led to a trio of large bank failures. For community banks, the vast majority of CDs originated at that time have since matured. For instance, at the end of the second quarter of 2024, 84.1% of CDs were set to mature a year later.

When CDs mature, most banks have to meet market rates to retain the deposits. CD rates began declining late in the third quarter of 2024 and far fewer institutions are offering the products at rates over 4%, but there has been less movement around the 3.5% level. The number of banks marketing one-year CDs over 3.5% included 1,053 institutions as of Aug. 8, up slightly from 1,048 as of June 27, but down from 1,079 as of March 28, 1,134 as of Dec. 27, 2024, and 1,266 as of Sept. 27.

Banks would like to cut deposit costs further but need to remain competitive with alternatives in the Treasury and money markets. The difference between the average fed funds rate — a proxy for those alternatives — and the community bank aggregate's cost of deposits widened slightly in the second quarter of 2025 from the prior quarter. Still, the gap remained far narrower than in 2023 and 2024.

Community banks likely need additional rate cuts by the Fed to record notable declines in deposit costs in future quarters. S&P economists do not expect the quarterly fed funds rate to decline by at least 25 basis points from current levels until the first quarter of 2026.

Deposits should grow further, but interest-bearing deposits will continue to grow at a quicker clip than non-interest-bearing deposits — banks' most prized source of funding.

Loan growth is expected to remain relatively weak as borrowers digest new trade policies that could change the cost of operating their businesses and households. The impact of tariffs arguably have not been felt as businesses increased inventories ahead of the landmark announcements in early April. Building inventories has allowed some businesses to maintain prices at pre-tariff rates, for now.

Weaker loan growth offers one silver lining for banks. If loan growth is slower, community banks' funding need would not be as great and could allow them to price deposits incrementally lower.

Credit quality still normalizing but potential signs of weaker economic conditions

Bankers maintained during second-quarter earnings season that credit quality remains benign, and the US economy has proven resilient in the face of uncertainty created by tariffs. However, credit quality has normalized with delinquencies rising off historical lows.

In the second quarter, nonaccrual loans at the community bank aggregate rose modestly to 0.67% of loans from 0.65% in the first quarter. That compares to 0.56% in the second quarter of 2024 and 0.43% in the second quarter of 2023.

Much of the concern in the investment community has related to commercial real estate loans. Some CRE borrowers face a double whammy of lower cash flows due to the rise of hybrid work and higher debt service stemming from increases in interest rates over the last few years. CRE nonaccruals have risen for 10 consecutive quarters at the community bank aggregate, but losses have often been less than many feared, in part due to well-funded distressed investors and private credit firms targeting the asset class. When banks have sold CRE loans — often in connection with an acquisition — sellers have recorded relatively modest discounts to par, ranging from 8% to 10%.

We expect related refinancing challenges to result in defaults and higher loss content in 2025 and 2026, with net chargeoffs continuing to rise from benign levels. We expect loss content to linger as banks provide borrowers with extensions in the face of some maturity walls.

Consumer delinquencies have risen from historical lows as well. While many community banks might not have sizable consumer or credit card portfolios, the consumer drives the economy. Currently, the US consumer is stretched — consumer spending and savings rates have slowed, and excess savings accumulated during the pandemic have been exhausted. Many businesses seem to have absorbed the cost of tariffs thus far, but it seems likely that some of those costs will eventually be passed on the consumer.

Community banks have assumed that greater stress lies ahead and have set aside considerable reserves for the most troubled projects. At the end of the second quarter of 2025, reserves rose modestly to 1.30% of loans. We expect further increases by year-end 2025 and small reserve builds in 2026 as economic uncertainty pushes banks to prepare for trouble ahead. Net chargeoffs should rise in 2025, but losses and the reserves required to fund them should serve as a modest headwind to earnings rather than a severe downturn.

Provisions are projected to rise to 14.9% of net revenue in 2025 and hold fairly steady at 14.4% in 2026. That level of provisions would represent a larger headwind to earnings than seen in recent periods, with provisions equating to 12.7% of net revenue through the first half of 2025, 13.0% in 2024 and 11.6% in 2023. On average, from 2014 to 2019, banks' provisions equated to 10.4% of net revenue.

Looking ahead

We think community banks would be wise to price greater risk into their loan portfolios and consider rethinking their growth goals. Bankers will likely find that strong loan growth will be tough to come by and could be prudent to avoid pressuring lenders aggressively at this point in the cycle.

Rather than fighting to meet their organic growth goals, bank managers should move in the other direction and encourage lenders to tighten pricing and lending standards, increase debt service coverage ratios and move away from their least creditworthy borrowers.

While lower loan volumes may reduce earnings in the near term, avoiding risk today could pay dividends later and help banks minimize credit losses. The approach could also help institutions to improve their funding bases, which represent the true value of a banking franchise.

A stronger funding base and continued strength in credit quality would be rewarded by investors and appeal to potential acquirers. Banks that are willing to take a step back and operate more cautiously now will also have a greater opportunity to continue growing, both organically and through acquisitions, when the next downturn occurs.

While M&A activity has remained relatively slow, the pace of deal activity seems poised to increase. A friendlier regulatory environment has brought back serial acquirers, and the combination of modestly higher credit costs and slower loan growth could create more willing sellers. Greater interest from institutions looking to partner with buyers could come as bank valuations have recovered from the lows in April, setting the stage for much stronger dealmaking activity.

Scope and methodology

The outlook discussed in this article is based on a proprietary S&P Global Market Intelligence model that utilizes the actual results of nearly 10,000 active and historical commercial and savings banks and savings and loan associations. The outlook is based on management commentary, discussions with industry sources, regression analysis, and asset and liability repricing data disclosed in banks' quarterly call reports. While taking into consideration historical growth rates, the analysis often excludes the significant volatility experienced in the years around the credit crisis.

The outlook is subject to change, perhaps materially, based on adjustments to the consensus expectations for interest rates, unemployment and economic growth. The projections can be updated or revised at any time as developments warrant, particularly when material changes occur.

data.title: RQI: Own The Real Estate Sector With This High-Yield CEF
data.description:
Overview

I've always been fascinated with the ability to maintain exposure to real estate through the stock market, without actually owning any physical properties. High-yield assets like Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) make it possible for investors to collect a sizeable income stream from real estate, without having to deal with the issues and headaches that come with being a landlord. When I previously covered RQI, I issued a buy rating due to the fund's resilience during this period of elevated interest rates. Since the time of my last coverage, RQI has released an updated annual report, and we seem to be getting closer to interest rate cuts.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RQI's share price has declined by about 7.1%. The fund did manage to recover from the market's decline earlier in the year, but the fund has still struggled to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return offsets the price decline but still remains under 1%. The main appeal of RQI is the generous starting dividend yield of 7.7% and monthly payouts, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a stream of reliable income generation. RQI has been able to maintain consistent distributions for a decade now.

After the most recent Fed meeting, it seems like we are finally closer to seeing interest rate cuts implemented. The fund utilizes leverage and is exposed to a sector that is heavily reliant on debt, so interest rate cuts can serve as a growth catalyst for the fund. Therefore, I believe that this is a great time to accumulate shares of RQI, which allows investors to collect a high yield while the recovery of the real estate sector takes place. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's updated valuation and reporting.

Updated Valuation

Since RQI is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. Therefore, RQI currently trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 3.11%. For reference, RQI has traded at an average discount to NAV of 4.88% over the last three-year period. To better conceptualize the current discount level in relation to its ten-year history, I added the red line to the graph below.

Despite RQI trading at a lower discount than its average, I still believe that RQI offers an attractive value proposition. After assessing hundreds of different closed end funds, I've noticed that the price discount or premium is usually a direct reflection of the underlying performance. With that said, I believe that RQI has demonstrated several traits that justify the fund eventually trading in the premium to NAV territory. Some of these traits include:

  • Providing capital appreciation over a ten year span.
  • Keeping distributions unchanged for a decade straight.
  • NAV remained steady throughout periods of market uncertainty.
  • Diverse holdings that span across the sub-sectors of real estate.

In an effort to get better clarity into the internal performance of the fund, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.10 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.45 per share. Looking back at prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have declined from prior years, but I attribute this to the lower operating spread related to higher interest rates. Since the fund utilizes leverage, the higher interest rate means that RQI needs to allocate a greater portion of its earnings to service its debt. Therefore, I anticipate that lower interest rates will improve net investment income levels.

Similarly, RQI has demonstrated that it is capable of providing a sufficient level of net realized gains when market conditions are favorable. The only issue is that higher interest rates have made it more difficult for the fund to capture the growth of underlying assets. The real estate sector continues to be one of the worst performing industries over the last few years. Due to the weaker net realized gains, RQI's NAV growth has remained fairly limited, which was a big criticism at the time of my last coverage. For reference, here are the updated year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $13.17 per share
  • 2021: $18.27 per share
  • 2022: $12.26 per share
  • 2023: $13.05 per share
  • 2024: $12.83 per share
  • 2025: $12.90 per share

As we can see in the chart below, RQI's share price has struggled to regain positive momentum back above its pre-pandemic level since interest rates were hiked throughout 2022 and 2023. Conversely, RQI was able to see its share price rapidly increase throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels. Therefore, I believe that future interest rate cuts will also help stimulate a recovery in the real estate sector.

The market seems to be anticipating interest rate cuts to happen as soon as September. A lower cost of debt will help underlying positions by improving operating margins and may lead to these businesses shifting back to a growth perspective. A lower cost of debt may increase the likelihood of RQI's holdings to pursue different growth initiatives such as acquisitions, development of new properties, or even value-add renovations to existing properties. Therefore, I feel comfortable accumulating shares at the current levels.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, RQI has total managed assets of $2.4B that are spread across 195 different holdings. The fund utilizes a blend between common stocks, preferred stocks, and other equity securities as a way to provide attractive total returns from the real estate sector. Additionally, RQI maintains exposure to a diverse range of different sub-sectors of real estate, which helps reduce any sort of concentration risk. The breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common/Preferred Stock: 82%
  • Fixed Income Securities: 18%

Looking at the breakdown by sector, we can see that the largest portion of the portfolio is allocated to Telecommunications at 14% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Health Care and Corporate Bonds at 12% and 10% of assets, respectively. RQI's wide range of sector exposure makes it easy for investors to instantly diversify their capital amongst some of the strongest REITs in the world.

The top ten holdings reveal many recognizable large-cap names within. For instance, Welltower (WELL) is the largest position at 9.1% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other large-cap REITs such as American Tower Corp. (AMT), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), Prologis (PLD), and Crown Castle (CCI) just to name a few. However, we must remember that RQI puts emphasis on income generation from its holdings, so this requires RQI to occasionally sell off portions of the upside movement to fuel distributions.

Additionally, the same annual report reveals that RQI actually implements a small amount of option writing as a way to help boost the income received from underlying positions. The inclusion of options will also limit the appreciation that can be experienced from underlying holdings. Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that RQI should only be utilized by investors looking to prioritize income generation, rather than maximizing total return potential.

Risk Profile

Interest rates will always be one of the primary driving forces for a real estate fund like RQI. While markets are anticipating a cut to drive the valuation of these types of funds higher, there are still some unknowns. For instance, there's no guarantee that the Fed will implement interest rate cuts that are large enough to have a positive impact on RQI. For instance, there were several interest rate cuts that were implemented during the tail end of 2024. Despite these cuts, the trajectory of RQI's share price remained unchanged.

There's always a chance that the Fed takes it slow and implements smaller rate cuts of 25 basis points. The Fed is likely to continue observing macroeconomic data such as inflation, the unemployment rate, and shifts in consumer spending habits. Additionally, the full impact of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined. I think it's possible that we see a scenario where the Fed only implements one or two small cuts, in an effort to avoid cutting rates to prematurely.

Additionally, RQI utilizes leverage as part of its operating strategy in an effort to amplify earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to approximately 29.34% of assets, which I consider to be fairly aggressive during this period of elevated interest rates. On a positive note, 81% of RQI's total leverage is structured on a fixed rate, meaning that higher interest rates don't actually impact the interest expense that RQI needs to pay out. However, 19% of RQI's total leverage is structured on a variable rate basis and has a cost of 5.2%.

The weighted average term on the fixed financing is for 2.2 years. However, I imagine that interest rates will still be elevated in 2.2 years, so RQI may need to refinance this debt at a higher cost at some point in the future. However, it's still very early to estimate where interest rates will be. I just think the overall use of leverage is something that investors should continue to monitor going forward.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.7%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.96 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, RQI has been able to keep payouts the same for the last decade. Thankfully, RQI's stronger-performing years have generated a sufficient level of income to continue supporting distributions at their current rate. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: ($0.40) per share
  • 2021: $6.06 per share
  • 2022: ($4.81) per share
  • 2023: $1.75 per share
  • 2024: $0.74 per share
  • 2025: $0.55 per share

Unfortunately, I feel that it's difficult to get an accurate reading on RQI's earnings by looking at the last few years. Interest rates remaining near their decade highs have continued to skew the fund's performance. I think performance will improve over time as interest rates are cut, but it will require patience from investors. However, keeping the distribution consistent for ten years is a commendable achievement, and I think this makes RQI a great choice for investors seeking a stream of reliable and consistent dividend income.

It's also worth mentioning that distributions received from RQI aren't the most tax-efficient. Based on the latest Section 19(a) notice, the majority of distributions paid YTD have come from the combination of net investment income and net realized short-term capital gains. These two methods of funding are typically classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in RQI may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on RQI at this time as I anticipate that future interest rate cuts will improve optimism of the real estate sector and lead to a positive shift in momentum. RQI still trades at a discount to NAV valuation, which also reinforces that this can be an attractive time to accumulate shares. The fund provides a diverse range of exposure to a ton of different sub-sectors within the real estate sectors, making it an efficient way to capture the growth of the entire sector. The fund has managed to keep distributions consistent for a decade, reinforcing that it's a great option for income investors seeking stability.
